title: Total Parenteral Nutrition (TPN): Safe Administration and Monitoring
subtitle: The nurse safely administers TPN — a hypertonic, high-alert solution given through a central line, run on a pump, never stopped abruptly, with close glucose and infection monitoring.

patient.name: TPN Administration
patient.admitDate: The nurse administers and monitors total parenteral nutrition using central access, an infusion pump, and aseptic technique.

vitals.temp: No other meds/blood in TPN lumen

labs.v: Run at the ordered rate only.
labs.r: No speeding up to catch up.

nursesNote: The nurse administers total parenteral nutrition as a high-alert therapy. Because the solution is hypertonic and high in dextrose, it is given only through a central venous catheter and run on an infusion pump at the exact ordered rate — the rate is never sped up to 'catch up' or slowed down. Before hanging, the nurse verifies the order and additives with a second nurse per policy, inspects the bag for cloudiness, precipitate, or oil separation, and uses strict aseptic technique at a dedicated lumen that is not used for other medications, blood, or CVP readings. TPN is never stopped abruptly; if the next bag is delayed, 10% dextrose (D10W) is hung to prevent rebound hypoglycemia. The nurse checks blood glucose every 4-6 hours, changes the bag and tubing every 24 hours, and monitors daily weight, intake and output, and electrolytes (watching for refeeding syndrome in malnourished patients) along with signs of infection.
providerOrders: Administer TPN through a central line via infusion pump at the ordered rate
Verify the solution and additives with a second nurse before hanging
Do not stop TPN abruptly; hang D10W if the next bag is delayed
Check blood glucose every 4-6 hours and treat per sliding scale
Change the bag and tubing every 24 hours using aseptic technique

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What is true about TPN administration?

opts.text: TPN is hypertonic and must be given through a central venous catheter
opts.rat: Too concentrated for a peripheral vein.

opts.text: TPN is run on an infusion pump at the prescribed rate
TPN can be stopped abruptly without any patient risk
Blood glucose is monitored regularly during TPN
Other medications can be piggybacked into the TPN line to save access
The bag and tubing are changed every 24 hours
Verify the solution and additives with a second nurse per policy
Inspect the bag and discard it if cloudy or showing precipitate
If the next bag is delayed, hang 10% dextrose to prevent hypoglycemia
Speed up the rate to catch up if the infusion falls behind
Use strict aseptic technique at the central line hub
Monitor daily weight, intake/output, and electrolytes

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order the steps to hang a new TPN bag

opts.text: Verify the order, bag, and additives with a second nurse
Inspect the solution for cloudiness, precipitate, or separation
Perform hand hygiene and use aseptic technique at the line hub
Connect to the dedicated lumen and set the pump to the ordered rate
Check blood glucose and document a baseline
Giving TPN through a peripheral IV to save the central line
Running TPN on an infusion pump at the ordered rate
Hanging D10W when the next TPN bag is delayed
Slowing the rate way down to make a bag last longer
Using a dedicated lumen for TPN only
Drawing a CVP reading from the TPN lumen
Changing the TPN tubing every 24 hours
Checking blood glucose every 4-6 hours

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: The next TPN bag is delayed — order the response
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Do not abruptly stop the current infusion
Notify pharmacy/provider that the next bag is needed
Hang 10% dextrose (D10W) at the ordered rate
Monitor the patient's blood glucose closely
Resume TPN as soon as the new bag is available
Infusing TPN through a central line on a pump
Stopping TPN suddenly when it runs out
Verifying the bag with a second nurse before hanging
Piggybacking an antibiotic into the TPN lumen
Monitoring glucose and electrolytes regularly
Speeding up the rate to catch up after a delay
Changing the bag and tubing every 24 hours
Hanging a cloudy bag with visible precipitate
